item.title: I ADMIT IT OKAY?!!
item.description: This is how it is explaining to people what I do for a living Ahahaha!!! I’m trying to branch out okay! It’s really hard trying to explain, whether it be to strangers I meet, family, new friends etc…it’s really hard. On every date I went on I didn’t have a problem telling people though, cuz I felt it better for them to know straight away ahahaha. But yeah no I really love drawing pinup art. I decided I really enjoyed making short form comics aswell, hence why my comic account was born. It’s still early days, I’m trying to juggle commissions, patreon and my new comics…I’m always interested in new projects. I definitely don’t have the stamina of other artists My comic instagram and p@treon https://www.instagram.com/miaminicomics https://www.patreon.com/miaminicomics b00ba accounts: Extra https://www.patreon.com/miaormoa Dono and commissions: https://ko-fi.com/miagabriella Instagram: https://www.instagram.com/miaormoa
item.dc:creator: miaormoa

item.title: It's about time
item.description: I'd like to preface that I'm starting on Testosterone. It took me some time to realize what was going on with myself because there's really not much out there about FtM compared to MtF, if y'all could share some masc stuff I'd appreciate it! FP edit: oh wow, this was... A lot more than I expected! Thank you all so much for the well wishes and open arms!!

item.title: Don't look up his cat's name
item.dc:creator: pilomotor

item.title: Touch of the ‘tism
item.description: I’m about 10 years older than my fellow undergrads in pre-med due to my childhood being straight torture and spending the bulk of my twenties getting myself together and quitting my addictions/self-medicating. Went out to lunch with my class and favorite professor for the end of the year fun. None of my close friends showed up, so I had to sit with people I know but am not close with. I tried my best to fit in and have a good time with them, but I noticed them snap chatting each other and laughing while awkwardly looking at me and looking away and not sharing the joke with me several times after I spoke. I thought this feeling of being the weird left-out kid would pass after high school and learning so much in therapy, but it still hurts like hell to be othered by people I would never be mean to. Thanks to anyone who read this. I’ll be okay, because at the end of the day I don’t really care what a bunch of 21 year-olds think of me, but I wish it didn’t hurt to so much to just exist in this world. #autism #adhd #depression_sucks #cptsd
item.dc:creator: naray0Terror

item.dc:creator: nintendolunchbox

item.title: Terrified
item.description: A few weeks ago I had an MRI for something different that found this. A 2cm abnormal mass on my cerebellum. Neurosurgeon isn't sure what it is yet and I'm getting another MRI today. I'm a super hard stick on account of having hereditarily bad veins. So, I hate needles. Wife doesn't deal with stress. At all. So, I'm doing this all alone. Anyways, I just needed to vent a little bit. Gotta keep going because that's all I can do.

item.title: I GOT MARRIED
item.description: If you saw my previous posts you may have remembered the date but me and Nora got married April 20th!!! Luckily nothing went wrong and my parents didn't try to break in (they got banned for homophobia) I'm so bloody happy!!!!

item.title: Really struggling right now
item.description: I was a stay-at-home dad for ten years. I'm working full-time now, but at a starting salary. She's a business executive with a good salary. She's refusing to pay child support or spousal support. She gave notice to our landlord, forcing us to move out (I can't afford the rent on my own). Basically she wants me homeless, with no access to the children I raised (they're 12 & 14). The kids are struggling emotionally with all this. I have a lawyer (paid for by legal aid, thank God), so no need to offer legal advice. I just needed to vent. My budget for rent is zero and I'm in a REALLY expensive part of the world -- though since my budget is zero it wouldn't really matter where I live. The law is clear and is on my side, but she's making every inch of it a battle. I've heard stories of longtime lovers becoming bitter enemies overnight, and now it's happening to me. Still struggling to accept that it's even happening.
item.dc:creator: Huor

item.title: Several Imgurians were kind enough to share their memories of a loved one they lost to AIDS. I made this post to remember them.
item.description:
Recap: I make educational posts using meme formatting. . Three weeks ago, I finished a two part post about HIV/AIDS. I also made a couple of posts looking for people who lived during that time, a post about the divide between the LGBTQ+ community and evangelical Christian politicians and a post providing some context for the younger generation. . Here are the links: HIV/AIDS Part 1: Understanding the Virus: https://imgur.com/gallery/qFnjuLe Looking for ACT UP Protestors: https://imgur.com/gallery/rYaXfPy LGBTQ+ vs/ Evangelical Christian Politicians: https://imgur.com/gallery/rAnN2bM Some Context into AIDS for Younger People: https://imgur.com/gallery/Qu4D2IF HIV/AIDS Pt. 2: Understanding the Massive Social and Historical Impact of the Virus: https://imgur.com/gallery/DzzkZMh . During these posts, many of you shared memories of their loved ones lost to AIDS. I decided to make a post dedicated to their memory. I asked others if they wanted to add to the list, and some of you did. I never forgot. I waited three weeks because I wanted to give everyone an opportunity to include someone - and a few folks needed some time to rummage around to find a photo of their loved one (old photo albums can be hard to find sometimes). Now I’m making this post dedicated to their memory. Thank you all for sharing your loved ones with me. If you didn’t get a chance to share one before I made this post, PLEASE feel free to do so in the comments! Your memories, in this post or not, are welcome here! . Also, because Imgurians shared their memories with me on certain subs, I’ve tagged those same ones. They might normally not belong to these tags, but I hope you can forgive it for this particular post. I tried to make some of these memories into memes to make this post a little more dynamic than a list of screenshots. However, some of these are screenshots because I either didn’t know HOW to make it a meme, or it was too dark for me to feel comfortable making it a meme. . However, before I begin this post, I want to add a beginning note due to personal experience in one of my previous posts about AIDS…
I apologize if I seem harsh about this sentiment, but after getting chewed out for using “apart” instead of “a part” in one of my posts about AIDS, I’m not interested in seeing a repeat of that in this post. Keep whatever grammatical and spelling errors that might be prevalent in this post to yourself. Now is not the time. These are people who are sharing a treasured memory of their loved ones, not an English 101 Class. You (hopefully) wouldn’t correct someone’s grammar at a funeral, so don’t do it now.
The person in this photo is David Kirby, an AIDS activist who passed away from the disease in 1990. He allowed photojournalist Therese Frare to take these photos of him actively dying from AIDS and his family saying goodbye to him. He died minutes after this photo was taken. Source: https://www.life.com/history/behind-the-picture-the-photo-that-changed-the-face-of-aids/
Full Quote: My angel and brother was diagnosed in '88. He was the only member of his social circle who survived to see 1990. His physicians called him 'The Miracle Man'. He not only lived, but once AZT was replaced with HAART and then more modern drug regimens, he thrived. He even completed a few ironmans. Undetectable for 15 years. I was in awe of him. He was my hero. - @asaruthismenother
I'm a 44 year old with hemophilia. I lost countless friends and family alike to HIV/AIDS. I was probably 6 when my uncle Danny started getting sick. He was a guitarist and had just fallen in love when he found out he had got HIV. His wife stayed with him until he died in 1990. To this day it was the saddest funeral I've ever attended. My grandfather never really got over it and lived with visible pain and heartbreak until he past about a decade later. I don't have a picture of Danny as an adult, when he was sick, but I do have one of him as a kid. He was what was known as a "poster boy" for kids with hemophilia - The picture is from 1965, and he's standing there in crutches with a giant grin on his face (a grin he had until he died) - and I think a lot about how he'd be dead in less than 30 years from taking medicine that he needed to survive. Something *I* needed to survive. If Danny could get sick, I could get sick... but then I didn't, not from HIV anyway - so I've lived with survivors guilt for decades now. It's impacted by the fact that kids that I knew at the hospital also died, and hearing the stories from my social worker of having to inform parents is just so incredibly heartbreaking. - @CroweFM
Full Quote: Both pt.1 and 2 were incredibly hard to read. It sucks how our government treated people with this disease back then. I lost an uncle in 1996 to AIDS. It hit him real quick after he caught it. Took a heavy toll on my mom after he passed. I hope a cure can be found soon… - @SNBeyond2016
- @JoeyG1207
Full Quote: Ross caught the virus in his late teens as a sex worker. It took his life at 30 in the late 90s. There's the trans woman I worked with in the mid-90s who had to leave owing to HIV dementia in her 40s. More trans women in the 90s I knew who did sex work after their transition meant they could no longer continue in their careers. There's the Gay Olympian I met in 2000 who I hope is still with us - he partied very hard indeed before the Games. - @CardboardEmpress
Full quote: My good friend's little brother died at a Ronald McDonald house, at the age of 11 from getting aids from blood transfusion, as a hemophiliac. I was there when he passed. He was just a child, and made me promise to take him to prom. Paul David should be here. That was 1989. All im saying to Regan is the devil. - @FreddyFender
- @jonhamsandwich11 Edit: I just learned that his name was Andy!
Full Quote: He was in an early wave and died fast, a piece of the quilt is devoted to him. He was beloved by 5 siblings, 3 step siblings, mother, various extended family, and a leader in the gay and drag community in 1980s Dallas when it was dangerous to be out. He liked all household items in multiples of 8. Was a heck of a creative designer and was always up to take out straight women friends for a night of dancing when they didn't feel safe on his own. His funeral was the party of the century. - @Weareallprimates
- @zoltarpanaflex
Full Quote: My mom's best friend growing up was kicked out by his parents for being gay when he was still a teenager. He had to turn to sex work to survive and unfortunately succumbed to AIDS not long after I was born. I was born during the height of the AIDS pandemic/panic, but my parents let him visit us and hold me as a baby rather than fearing him. I guess the first time they let him hold me, he cried because it was so meaningful. I don't think his story is unique. So many LGBTQ+ individuals were victimized from all sides: their families disowned them, their government didn't care about them, and many "loving" Christians cheered their deaths. I'm glad my parents were able to offer kindness to him in his final days. - @KitsuneMitsukai
- @TheHolyFatman
Full Quote: My beloved Godfather. I was proud of him, he fought his heroin addiction and was clean after years of hell but unfortunately lost his battle against aids, the new Antiretroviral tritherapy came to late for him. He was the younger brother of my mother. I was raised with him, he was like an older brother for me, always cheerful for me even when he was walking dark places in his mind and body, he's a model for me and I am grateful to have known him. The hardest part for me was hearing horrendous things in school about people suffering aids and not being able to fight back at them because at this time it was a taboo, to be clear my family was not biggot or anything like that, it is a very open minded family We were not ashamed that he was hiv positive but our surrending force us to keep this as a secret because people were so uninformed about it that it was impossible to live as peacefully as possible if it was known unfortunately, and it's break my heart just to remember - @EvermoreNevermore
- @thelatebillstickers
Full Quote: Colleen Macintyre 1996, her lover Gerry Oulette got the illness from tainted blood and passed it to her. I played music with her for 5 years, touring Germany and playing CBGB's. She had talent and beauty and when she got real drunk, her eyes crossed. - @hammeronmycalf
- @CheugieHowserMD
Full Quote: Late 1982, met friends as usual at the club. 1 had been dieting for a couple of months. Smiles, partying. Next week he's a no show. Phone was unanswered (no mobiles then) so a couple of the guys went round to his "House by the sea". (He lived in a tower block near the canal lol) No answer at the door. When they looked thru the letterbox it was empty. Called his Mom, she said she couldn't talk to us. We were told of his death 3 months later. Advised to be careful, no test back then. He was, by his own admission, a total slut. Loved meeting new guys, partied hard, a flamboyant, bright shooting star in the night, taken by a cruel disease. He didn't want us to see him sick. His Mom said he wanted us to remember him as the gorgeous one of the group. There was no treatment then, it was a death sentence. He was the epiphany of a gay 80's guy and we truly loved and missed him. The weird thing was that we thought of Aids as an American disease. Our 1st case was Dec 1981? This was 82. My male bff was gay and i was his cover story. Having relations under the age of 21 was illegal for men then. A wonderful, flamboyant mix of people i was honored to know. He said he'd keep safe by avoiding "Yanks and sailors". Warnings and ads to the general population didn't start here (UK) til '86. He'd been gone 4 years by then. We just didn't know anything. - @joemac65
Full Quote: I graduated high school in 1974 in a town northwest of Chicago. My husband and I moved to 60 south of L.A. in 1984. That same year, I went to my 10 year high school reunion and discovered that 11 classmates had died from AIDS by then. None of these friends talked about being gay openly *ever* b/c of the physical and career ending danger. Reagan was doing nothing which enraged me. I had babies at the time but I and other friends from school began a letter writing campaign via the New York group - @FosterMomForKittens
Full Quote: My dad is a minister. When my former grade 3 teacher was dying of AIDS he requested my father perform the funeral. Despite opposition from within the church, my dad did it anyway. The next day during service my dad wore a rose on his lapel in honor of Mr Thomas. Although we no longer share a faith, seeing my community (I'm a lesbian) in church and my dad standing up to hate remains one of the proudest moments of my life. He was a very kind teacher. Very engaging with a group of grade 3s. I remember him reading to us and treating us like our thoughts mattered. - @DaughterOfSappho
Full Quote: RIP "Aunt" Paul. He died in 1987 after his boyfriend cheated on him and brought HIV home with him. Just list "''Aunt' Paul: A hard man is good to find" - @barbarian818
Full Quote: I lost my beautiful uncle and his partner in 85, 87 respectively. Medical field and humans treated patients like garbage. He was a precious soul that was dearly loved. I am blessed that all I have ever known is to love and let love. I can't imagine not loving and missing him. Education is KEY. Uncle Steve was and always will be a beautiful fabric in my journey. He help mold me into who l am as a person. He taught me humility and true love. - @shanasphone350
[Michael and Kimberly are] My parents. Dad was in a hit and run accident in summer of '86, lost his left leg and needed a blood transfusion. They weren't testing blood bank blood yet. He was unknowingly given HIV+ blood. He passed the infection, again unknowingly, to my mother. He was a volunteer firefighter, even after he lost his leg. Dad was diagnosed when my mom was about 3 months pregnant with me.Mom was diagnosed a few days later. They told her to terminate her pregnancy, said that there was no chance I'd be HIV- and wouldn't live to see my 5th birthday. Obviously, that didn't happen as here I am! My father passed away just after we finally got the news that I was HIV-. Mom was already involved in awareness and outreach at this point, using her contacts from being a social worker for the state of Ohio to help make things better for other HIV+ folks in our area. She spent a lot of time helping to breakdown the stigma that it was something only gay people would get. She gave talks at schools, from elementary to university. Anyone who'd listen really. She helped start programs to help fund the medication for people who couldn't afford it, was even involved in some drug trials herself as well. I could go on for hours about all the things she did in just a few short years. - @GlitterBiscuits
- @RayneOfSalt
EDIT: I’ve been getting a lot of private messages with people sharing their loved one with me for a future post. I’ve finished my AIDS posts, and don’t intend to return to it. But thank you for sharing your loved one with me! I’ve been encouraging people to share their loved ones in the comments of this post so you can still remember them and have them acknowledged. Even if I didn’t include them in a post, your loved one is still important - I just unfortunately lack the ability to make a post that includes EVERYONE.
Memorial Website: https://www.aidsmemorial.org/quilt
item.dc:creator: OlivetheNerd
